annotations.label: Center squeeze (definition)

anchor.snippet: A center squeeze is a kind of spoiler effect shared by rules like the two-round system

annotations.note: Social choice theory voting concept; Mathlib has no voting/spoiler-effect theory.

annotations.label: Center squeeze as spoiler effect

anchor.snippet: Center squeezes are a kind of spoiler effect in which the majority-preferred candidate is eliminated before the final round

annotations.note: No spoiler-effect or candidate-elimination concepts exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Black's median-voter theorem

anchor.snippet: the candidate who appeals most to the median voter will be the majority-preferred candidate

annotations.note: Black's median voter theorem is not formalized; Mathlib has no social choice theory.

annotations.label: Meaning of "center"

anchor.snippet: refers to candidates who are close to the center of public opinion

annotations.note: Informal political-spectrum notion with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Center-squeeze incentivizes polarization

anchor.snippet: Voting systems that suffer from the center-squeeze effect incentivize candidates to avoid the political center

annotations.note: Game-theoretic claim about candidate incentives; absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Center squeeze in multiple-round systems

anchor.snippet: Center squeeze is a major feature of two-party systems using primaries or other multiple-round systems

annotations.note: Empirical claim about voting systems with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Score voting selects central candidate

anchor.snippet: score voting will always select the candidate closest to some central tendency of the voter distribution

annotations.note: Score voting is not modeled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cardinal methods converge on Condorcet winner

anchor.snippet: spoilerproof cardinal methods tend to behave like approval voting and thus converge on the Condorcet winner

annotations.note: No Condorcet or cardinal-voting concepts exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bias toward bland candidates is uncommon

anchor.snippet: a bias in favor of bland, inoffensive, or unknown candidates

annotations.note: Qualitative voting-bias claim with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Alphabet Land Condorcet winner

anchor.snippet: is the majority-preferred (Condorcet) winner

annotations.note: Illustrative election example; no Condorcet machinery in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First-preference plurality outcome

anchor.snippet: with 35.9% of voters choosing them as their favorite

annotations.note: Worked plurality-tally example, not a Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Ranked-choice voting

anchor.snippet: Ranked-choice voting (RCV) tries to address vote-splitting in FPP by replacing it with a series of FPP elections, with the loser being eliminated in each round

annotations.note: Ranked-choice voting procedure is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: RCV swing between extremes

anchor.snippet: RCV thus fails to have a substantial moderating impact, instead only causing a swing from one extreme to the other

annotations.note: Qualitative RCV behavior claim with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: 1991 Louisiana gubernatorial election

anchor.snippet: Buddy Roemer was squeezed out of the election, leaving corrupt Edwin Edwards and white supremacist David Duke in the runoff

annotations.note: Real-world election case study, not mathematical content.

annotations.label: 2007 French presidential election

anchor.snippet: The 2007 French presidential election was seen as an example of a center squeeze

annotations.note: Historical election example with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: 2012 Egyptian presidential election

anchor.snippet: vote-splitting among three leading moderate, non-Islamist candidates caused them all to be eliminated in the first round

annotations.note: Historical election example, not mathematical content.

annotations.label: 2022 Alaska special election

anchor.snippet: The 2022 Alaska special election seat was seen as an example of a center squeeze

annotations.note: Real-world election example with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: 2009 Burlington mayoral election

anchor.snippet: serves as an example of a four-candidate center squeeze

annotations.note: Historical election case study, not mathematical content.

annotations.label: Burlington overall preference ranking

anchor.snippet: This leads to an overall preference ranking of

annotations.note: Specific election preference tally, not a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: 2016 US presidential election

anchor.snippet: Another possible example is the 2016 United States presidential election

annotations.note: Real-world election example with no Mathlib formalization.

anchor.snippet: for creating a center squeeze in the 2024 presidential election
